name: United States
summary: The United States is the world's largest economy by nominal GDP and the dominant global military power. It leads a network of alliances spanning Europe and Asia-Pacific, while facing internal political polarization, fiscal pressures, and strategic competition from China. Its global influence is exercised through economic leverage, military presence, and soft power — though the legitimacy and sustainability of this role is increasingly contested.

politicalStability.note: High domestic polarization; institutional stress since 2020 election cycle

economicDirection.note: Strong services and tech sectors; high debt-to-GDP ratio is a long-term concern

investmentAttractiveness.note: Deep capital markets, rule of law, dollar dominance; protectionist trend emerging

geopoliticalRisk.note: US-China rivalry intensifying; NATO commitments under strain

educationQuality.note: World-class universities; K-12 system uneven by region/income

healthcareQuality.note: High quality of care; access deeply unequal; highest per-capita cost globally

technologyInvestment.note: Dominant in AI, semiconductors, defense tech; export controls expanding

relationships.summary: Strategic rivalry across trade, technology, military, and geopolitical influence. Decoupling accelerating in semiconductors and AI supply chains.
Adversarial relationship deepened by Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine. US is largest single military supplier to Ukraine.
Closest ally. Shares intelligence (Five Eyes), nuclear cooperation (AUKUS), and strong diplomatic alignment on most issues.
Formal treaty ally since 1954 Manila Pact. Relationship strained by Thailand's 2014 coup and growing Chinese economic ties.

perspectives.view: The US-led rules-based international order has produced the longest period of great-power peace in modern history, lifted billions from poverty, and should be defended and reformed, not abandoned.

US foreign policy consistently prioritizes American interests over stated values. Selective application of human rights norms, support for authoritarian allies, and economic coercion undermine US credibility.
US hegemony is a destabilizing force that suppresses sovereign decision-making. NATO expansion and military encirclement provoke the conflicts it claims to prevent.
Military spending crowds out social investment. US overseas interventions have frequently caused civilian harm disproportionate to security gains.
The US has overextended itself defending allies who should fund their own security. Foreign entanglements distract from domestic renewal and border security.

historicalContext.summary: Founded as a revolutionary republic in 1776, the US expanded across North America through purchase, war, and displacement of indigenous peoples. It emerged from WWII as a global superpower and anchored the Western alliance against the Soviet Union. The Cold War's end left it as the sole superpower, a 'unipolar moment' that has been gradually eroding since the 2003 Iraq War, the 2008 financial crisis, and China's rise.

keyEvents.event: Emerges from WWII as dominant global power; establishes UN, IMF, World Bank
Soviet Union collapses; US enters unipolar era
Financial crisis; global confidence in US model shaken
Trump election signals nationalist turn; multilateral institutions under strain
Russia invades Ukraine; US leads Western response

strengths: World's deepest and most liquid capital markets
Dollar is global reserve currency — structural advantage
Dominant in AI, cloud, biotech, and defense
Strong rule of law and property rights
risks: Protectionist industrial policy increasing costs for importers
